story:
Bobby is bored in the back seat. He has stared at SpongeBob all morning. Outside, there’s only flat land, green grass, blue sky, and big cows Mama says are buffalo. He knows that’s wrong—buffalo have wings. She laughs when he says so. His sneakers thump-thump the back of her seat. She asks him to stop. He asks, “Are we there yet?” again. She suggests his tablet. He says it’s boring and makes him sleepy. She tells him to rest anyway and promises to wake him when they get there. He makes her promise. She promises.

Cool air and Mama’s hands wake him. The seatbelt clicks. “We’re here,” she says, setting him on the parking lot gravel. He blinks. More flat land. More grass. “But you said we were going there,” he says. She takes his hand and starts walking. “We did. Now we’re here. They have all sorts of things to do here.”

He digs his heels in. “But I wanted to go there.” She asks him not to whine and reminds him to use his big boy voice. “We just got here. We’ll look around. Maybe hot chocolate. Maybe ice cream. After we’ve looked around here, we’ll go there.” He asks if she promises. She promises.

Barely satisfied, Bobby follows between pens of smelly, noisy animals and tables of shiny things. He tries to understand. “So we’re doing this here and that there?” “Right,” she says, studying the shiny things. “Then why did you say we’d do this there?” “I didn’t,” she says. “I said we’d do this here.” Bobby frowns. “You’re lying,” he blurts, foot stomping. That tightness returns to Mama’s voice and hand. “That’s not a nice thing to say.” He insists he asked if they were there yet and she promised to wake him when they got there. “I did wake you,” she says. “We’re here.”

He sighs. Maybe Mama doesn’t know everything after all. He tries again: “We were here this morning.” She corrects him gently but firmly: “We were there this morning. Now we’re here.” He isn’t sure anymore. He mostly wants to be there. “Can we be done here? After ice cream?” She gives in. “Okay. Ice cream, then we’re done here. Chocolate? Strawberry?” “Strawberry!”

Sticky-faced and smiling, he finishes the pink scoop, smears on his yellow t-shirt like a medal. “Let’s go there,” Mama says, cheerful again.

Back in the car, green grass stretches to the blue sky. Clumps of wingless buffalo dot the field. He wonders if they used to have wings and people ate them. But the wings on his plate were tiny for such big cows. His tummy hurts. “Maybe it was the ice cream,” Mama says. It will pass, she promises. He thumps the seat again. “Are we there yet?” she hears, and suggests a video. He agrees, then asks again, “Are we there yet?”

Round and round go the wheels. Here becomes there, and there becomes here the moment they arrive. Mama keeps her promises as best she can. Bobby keeps checking. Between buffalo without wings, a tablet video, and the thump-thump-thump of sneakers, the road hums with a small boy’s first lesson in time and place: wherever you are, it’s here. Wherever you’re going, it’s there. And the second you get there, you’ll call it here and start asking, again, about there.
